app.name: kognit
app.tagline: The edge is in your mind, get your focus back.

notFound.message: Oops, we couldn't find this page

subtitles.signup: Start training focus and control under pressure.
subtitles.forgot: We'll send you a link to reset your password.

toasts.signupSuccessDescription: If the email is available, we sent you a confirmation link.
toasts.forgotSuccessDescription: If the email is registered, you'll get a link to reset your password.
toasts.genericSignup: We couldn't complete your signup. Try again.
toasts.genericForgot: We couldn't process the request. Try again.

landing.logoAlt: kognit logo, mental training app for poker players
landing.heroSubtitle: 90 seconds. One breath. Your best decision.
landing.footer: kognit · designed to use under pressure

onboarding.welcomeSubtitle: We already customized kognit with how you feel and what you want to achieve.
onboarding.questionSubtitle: You can pick more than one. It helps us personalize your resets.
onboarding.goalsTitle: What do you want to improve?

frustration.name: Frustration
frustration.description: When things don't go as expected

reasons.frustration: Frustration needs deep emotional processing: 90 seconds help you lower the activation and regain perspective.
reasons.fear: Fear requires immediate clarity. A quick reset redirects your attention without amplifying emotion.
reasons.overthinking: A quick reset breaks the mental loop: it interrupts the paralyzing analysis and gets you back into action.
reasons.overwhelm: Feeling overwhelmed needs immediate focus on the present. The quick mode recalibrates your scattered attention.
reasons.unmotivated: Low motivation is an energy problem. 90 seconds help you reconnect with purpose and meaning.
reasons.tilt: Tilt is extreme emotional activation. It takes time for your nervous system to fully calm down.

calmAnchor.title: Calm anchor
calmAnchor.subtitle: This space is for you to set a moment to come back to.
calmAnchor.infoAria: How to use the calm anchor

steps.title: Breathe deeply
steps.body: The main goal is to create separation between you and the emotion. This stops the "psychic entropy."

steps.title: Set the anchor
steps.body: As you say the phrase you're going to anchor, make a small physical gesture (like pressing your thumb). According to Robert Dilts, this is an "anchor": a specific stimulus that automatically evokes a previously associated inner state of calm. The phrase could remind you of a place, a person, a scent…

steps.title: Inject logic
steps.body: Use the phrase to reactivate your rational mind. When emotion runs high, the brain blocks higher functions; breathing and verbalizing lets you "inject logic" and regain your attention.

steps.title: Focus on the present
steps.body: Direct your psychic energy toward a small, immediate goal. Wellbeing appears when there's "order in consciousness," achieved by focusing attention on the here and now.

grounding.hint: Don't overthink the answer. Tap the first one that feels right.
grounding.footer: You're coming down from emotional mode into rational mode.

questions.q: Where are you sitting right now?
What object is closest to you?
What were you doing an hour ago?
What sound do you hear right now?

state.title: What are you feeling right now?
state.subtitle: Naming it is already regulating it. You can pick more than one.
state.notePlaceholder: Anything else you want to jot down (optional)

frustration: One hand doesn't define your session.
Go back to the decision, not the outcome.
anxiety: You don't need to recover anything right now.
impatience: Your next step is the only thing that matters.
Wait one round before returning to the pace.
fatigue: If your body asks for a break, listen to it.
Playing solid outweighs playing a lot.
overconfidence: Go back to your range by position.
The process is more reliable than the streak.
fear: Decide from the plan, not from fear.
The outcome doesn't play this hand: your read does.

tilt.defaultExitMessage: Come back with a clear head.

pulse.title: How wound up are you?
pulse.subtitle: Be honest. We'll compare at the end.

check.title: How are you now?
check.subtitle: It doesn't have to be zero. Just more stable than when you started.
check.feedbackSome: You brought the pulse down. Keep going.
check.feedbackNone: Sometimes just naming it is enough.

exit.title: Come back with a clear head

cards.lockedHint: Create an account to see more cards

calendar.quickAddSubtitle: What went through your head today?

fallbackNotes.title: NL50 Session — 2 hours
fallbackNotes.body: Good control in tough spots. Folded a set on a read river, the outcome didn't knock me off balance.

fallbackNotes.title: Post bad-beat recovery
fallbackNotes.body: Set vs runner-runner flush. Did 4-7-8 breathing and came back clean in 6 minutes.

fallbackNotes.title: Pre-session ritual
fallbackNotes.body: Visualized 3 key hands from yesterday. Set a stop-loss: 3 buy-ins.

avatar.invalidType: The file must be an image
avatar.tooLarge: The image can't be larger than 5MB

flowState.title: Flow state
flowState.criterion: Keep an activity where the challenge matches your skill for at least 20 minutes.
flowState.reference: The balance between challenge and skill creates total absorption.

firstAlly.title: First ally
firstAlly.criterion: Invite an accountability partner to track a habit together.
firstAlly.reference: Social commitment increases the odds of success.

fiveDayStreak.title: 5-day streak
fiveDayStreak.criterion: Log your habits for 5 days without missing twice in a row.
fiveDayStreak.reference: The "never miss twice" mantra protects consistency.

unstoppable.title: Nothing stops me
unstoppable.criterion: Successfully complete a hard task in an initially unfavorable environment.
unstoppable.reference: Reflects the "autotelic personality" that finds flow in difficulty.

twoMinuteRule.title: Two-minute rule
twoMinuteRule.criterion: Complete the simplified version of a new habit to beat initial resistance.
twoMinuteRule.reference: Standardizing the start matters more than optimizing the outcome.

caterpillarEffect.title: Caterpillar effect
caterpillarEffect.criterion: Identify and fix your most frequent mistake (raise your "worst level").
caterpillarEffect.reference: Real progress happens when you raise your performance floor.

onePercentBetter.title: 1% better daily
onePercentBetter.criterion: Log a small improvement or marginal gain in a specific skill.
onePercentBetter.reference: The accumulation of small gains creates huge changes over time.

closingRitual.title: Closing ritual
closingRitual.criterion: Run your work shutdown steps and say, even just to yourself, "day is closed."
closingRitual.reference: Frees the mind from the Zeigarnik effect (unfinished tasks) so you can rest.

calmAnchor.title: Calm anchor
calmAnchor.criterion: Use a conditioned physical gesture to calm yourself in a tense moment.
calmAnchor.reference: Anchoring lets you recall positive internal states at will.

reminders.label: Daily reminder
reminders.enableHint: A gentle nudge to check in with yourself.
reminders.quote: "Before you play, listen to yourself. Your performance starts with your mental state."

preferences.label: Preferences
preferences.soundDefaultHint: Turns on audio when you enter Reset.

privacy.label: Privacy
privacy.text: Only you see your resets and private notes. Notes you mark as public show up in Community with your name. We never share your information with third parties.

deleteAccount.label: Delete account
deleteAccount.title: Delete account
deleteAccount.warning: This action erases your notes, reactions, messages, reset sessions, ritual entries and uploaded photos, and signs you out. It can't be undone.
deleteAccount.technicalNote: Due to a current technical limitation, your account record in the authentication system may be left without associated data instead of being fully deleted. If you need that, contact us separately.

toasts.deleteSuccess: Your account and your data were deleted
toasts.deleteError: Something went wrong, but we'll sign you out anyway

empty.title: No shared moments yet
empty.subtitle: Be the first to share a reflection.

locked.title: Create an account to see the posts
locked.subtitle: Join the kognit community to see notes, react, and reply.

inbox.title: Messages
inbox.emptyMessages: You don't have any messages yet

settings.blockedNotice: You blocked this person. You can't send them messages.

block.title: Block {{name}}?
block.body: They won't be able to send you messages anymore. You can unblock them anytime.

unblock.title: Unblock {{name}}?
unblock.body: They'll be able to message you again.

delete.title: Delete this conversation?
delete.body: It's removed from your inbox. If {{name}} messages you again, it'll come back.

toasts.invalidImageType: The file must be an image
toasts.imageTooLarge: The image can't be larger than 5MB

cards.title: Identity, not outcomes
cards.message: Don't say "I'm trying to run," say "I'm a runner." It's easier to sustain a habit that aligns with who you believe you are. — James Clear
cards.action: Focus on your identity, not on your results.

cards.title: The two-minute rule
cards.message: Every new habit needs to be able to start in under two minutes. Standardize first, optimize later: what matters is getting started. — James Clear
cards.action: Shrink your next habit down to a version under 2 minutes.

cards.title: The habit loop
cards.message: Every habit follows 4 steps: cue (the trigger), craving (the motivation), response (the action), and reward (the satisfaction). — James Clear
cards.action: Identify the cue that triggers your next habit.

cards.title: Habit stacking
cards.message: Use the formula: "After [current habit], I will [new habit]." For example: "After I pour my coffee, I meditate for one minute." — James Clear
cards.action: Pick a habit you already have and attach a new one right after it.

cards.title: Your environment designs your habits
cards.message: Your environment generates cues all the time. If you want to eat healthy, put fruit in plain sight; if you want to read, leave the book on your pillow. — James Clear
cards.action: Make a cue for a good habit you want to build visible.

cards.title: Self-control is a myth
cards.message: Disciplined people don't have more willpower: they design their environment so they don't run into temptation. Avoiding it is easier than resisting it. — James Clear
cards.action: Remove a temptation from your environment instead of promising to resist it.

cards.title: Never miss twice
cards.message: Missing one day is a normal mistake. Missing two is the start of a new — and bad — habit. What matters is the immediate recovery. — James Clear
cards.action: If you missed yesterday, get back on track today, no excuses.

cards.title: Habit contract
cards.message: Sign a written commitment with someone who will hold you accountable. Knowing there's a social cost for failing keeps you consistent. — James Clear
cards.action: Tell someone your commitment and ask them to call you out if you slip.

cards.title: Make it invisible
cards.message: If your phone distracts you, leave it in another room. If you watch too much TV, unplug it. Removing the visual cue is the most effective way to break the cycle. — James Clear
cards.action: Get the cue for your worst habit out of sight today.

cards.title: The decisive moment
cards.message: Small choices, like putting on your workout clothes, decide whether the next two hours will be productive or not. — James Clear
cards.action: Identify the first decision of your block and resolve it in favor of the habit.

cards.title: Attention residue
cards.message: When you switch from one task to another, your attention doesn't travel whole: part of it stays stuck on the previous one and drags your performance down. — Sophie Leroy
cards.action: Mentally close out the previous task before starting the next one.

cards.title: The formula for quality work
cards.message: Quality = time invested × intensity of focus. If you work distracted, intensity drops and the result is poor no matter how many hours you put in. — Cal Newport
cards.action: Before you start, remove one concrete distraction from your environment.

cards.title: Deep work
cards.message: It's the activity done in a state of total concentration, free of distractions, that pushes your cognitive capacity to its limit and generates real value. — Cal Newport
cards.action: Block off 30 minutes with no notifications for a single task.

cards.title: Train your attention muscle
cards.message: Schedule fixed times to use the internet and stay disconnected the rest of the day. Resisting the urge for "a quick look" trains your brain against instant gratification. — Cal Newport
cards.action: Set a screen-time window today and stick to it outside that window.

cards.title: The Roosevelt technique
cards.message: Give a hard task a drastically short deadline and commit out loud to hitting it. The pressure pushes you toward extreme intensity. — Cal Newport
cards.action: Give your next task a shorter deadline than you'd like.

cards.title: Shutdown ritual
cards.message: Write down everything pending for tomorrow and close the day with a phrase said out loud, like "day complete." Your mind stops having to remember it. — Cal Newport
cards.action: Write down tomorrow's pending items and say out loud that the day is done.

cards.title: Learn to be bored
cards.message: If you escape boredom by grabbing your phone all the time, you teach your brain to get distracted. Tolerating boredom is what later lets you concentrate deeply. — Cal Newport
cards.action: Next time you get bored, don't grab your phone: sit with it for 2 minutes.

cards.title: The real limit of concentration
cards.message: Even experts can only sustain deep concentration for a maximum of 4 hours a day; a beginner burns out in a single hour. Don't push harder than your brain can actually process. — Anders Ericsson
cards.action: Schedule your most demanding focus block based on your real level, not the ideal one.

cards.title: The recent-graduate test
cards.message: Ask yourself how long it would take a smart recent graduate to learn to do that task. If the answer is "not long," it's shallow and doesn't generate real value. — Cal Newport
cards.action: Apply that test to your next task before prioritizing it.

cards.title: Batch it, don't check constantly
cards.message: Checking email every 10 minutes destroys your focus. Constant "semi-distraction" is the enemy of high performance: batch your checking into fixed blocks. — Cal Newport
cards.action: Set 2 or 3 fixed times a day to check messages.

cards.title: Breathe to think
cards.message: Faced with a strong emotion, a deep breath creates a physical separation between you and that emotion, giving you time to inject logic before you react. — Jared Tendler
cards.action: Take a deep breath before responding to something that upset you.

cards.title: Psychic entropy
cards.message: Without something external to focus on, the mind wanders on its own — and it tends to drift toward painful thoughts, frustrations, or grudges. — Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi
cards.action: If your mind is wandering aimlessly, give it a concrete focus on purpose.

cards.title: Nature as recharge
cards.message: Walking in a natural setting replenishes your reserves of directed attention, according to Attention Restoration Theory. — Marc Berman
cards.action: Go for a 10-minute walk outside, no screen.

cards.title: Productive meditation
cards.message: Pick a single, well-defined problem and think it through while you walk or do some routine physical activity. If your mind wanders, bring it back to the problem. — Cal Newport
cards.action: On your next walk, bring a single problem to think through.

cards.title: Physiology inventory
cards.message: Check in on your shoulders, your breathing, and your posture. Changing the body — posture and breath — changes the mental state. — Robert Dilts
cards.action: Do a shoulders, breathing, and posture check right now.

cards.title: Microflow
cards.message: Small gestures like whistling or drumming your fingers are "microflow": they maintain a minimal sense of mental order. Depriving yourself of them can make you irritable within hours. — Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi
cards.action: Don't hold back a small gesture that calms you — let it happen.

cards.title: Your life is your attention
cards.message: Your life is the sum of what you pay attention to. The quality of your experience depends on focusing your psychic energy on goals worth pursuing. — Winifred Gallagher
cards.action: Choose, right now, what you're going to pay attention to for the next hour.

cards.title: Breathe to activate
cards.message: If you feel low on energy, fast, deep breaths raise your alertness — the opposite of what you need when you're overactivated. — Jared Tendler
cards.action: If you're unmotivated, take 5 fast, deep breaths.

cards.title: Attention orders the mind
cards.message: Concentrating intensely on something leaves no room for worries or irrelevant noise. That's where the feeling of peace and control comes from. — Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi
cards.action: Choose one single thing to do right now, with full attention.

cards.title: Leisure with purpose
cards.message: Unstructured free time can generate restlessness. Give your leisure a goal — learning something, a sport — so your mind doesn't fall into chaos. — Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi
cards.action: Choose a small goal for your next bit of free time.

stress.name: Stress and Emotion Management
stress.tagline: Lower the emotion before you decide.

cards.title: Emotional threshold
cards.message: When emotion crosses a certain threshold, the brain shuts down higher-order thinking functions. You can't reason there: you have to lower the emotion first. — Jared Tendler
cards.action: If you're very worked up, don't decide yet: lower the intensity first.

cards.title: Reframing
cards.message: Ask yourself what positive intention is hidden behind that thought or fear — for example, "this fear is trying to protect me." Recognizing its function lets you look for a healthier alternative. — Robert Dilts
cards.action: Identify what your latest negative thought is trying to protect you from.

cards.title: Logic injection
cards.message: Have a powerful logical phrase written down beforehand — like "it's just variance, stick to your plan" — and read it when you feel like you're losing control. It reactivates your rational mind. — Jared Tendler
cards.action: Write your logic-injection phrase now, to use in a crisis.

cards.title: Act as if
cards.message: Visualize the goal already achieved and practice today the behaviors of that version of yourself. It stimulates the neurology of success. — Robert Dilts
cards.action: Take one concrete action today as the person you want to be.

cards.title: There's no failure, only data
cards.message: There's no such thing as failure, only feedback. Treat every mistake like a scientist: data to improve the next experiment. — James Clear / Robert Dilts
cards.action: Write down one concrete lesson from your last mistake, without judging yourself.

cards.title: Rewrite the question
cards.message: Change "what happens if I fail?" to "what do I need to do now to give myself the best shot at success?" Changing the question reduces uncertainty. — Jared Tendler
cards.action: Reframe your current worry with that second question.

cards.title: Anchoring
cards.message: Associate a physical gesture — like clenching a fist — with a memory of total calm. Repeating that gesture in moments of stress recovers part of that state. — Robert Dilts
cards.action: Choose your anchor gesture and practice it now with a calm memory.

cards.title: Entitlement tilt
cards.message: Believing that because you put in the effort you "deserve" to win generates tilt. Short-term results are variable by nature — accepting that releases the stress. — Jared Tendler
cards.action: Repeat: "today's result doesn't measure whether I deserved it."

cards.title: The map is not the territory
cards.message: Your beliefs aren't reality, they're your representation of it. If your current map is generating stress, expand it with a new perspective. — Alfred Korzybski
cards.action: Look for a second possible reading of the situation that's stressing you out.

cards.title: The intention behind the criticism
cards.message: Ask yourself what the person criticizing you is trying to protect or improve. Responding to that real need, instead of the attack, changes the whole conversation. — Robert Dilts
cards.action: Next time you're criticized, look for the intention before you react.

cards.title: The flow balance
cards.message: Flow appears at the exact point where the challenge matches your skill: not so easy that it bores you, not so hard that it distresses you. — Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi
cards.action: Raise or lower the difficulty of your current task until you hit that point.

cards.title: Deliberate practice
cards.message: Focus on one specific skill, repeat it, and seek immediate feedback. That's how real learning speeds up. — Anders Ericsson
cards.action: Pick a single skill to practice with feedback today.

cards.title: Mastery under pressure
cards.message: A skill is only mastered once it holds up under extreme pressure. If it breaks down under stress, there's still work to do. — Jared Tendler
cards.action: Test your newest skill in a real high-pressure situation.

cards.title: Goals with immediate feedback
cards.message: Flow needs goals that tell you, second by second, whether you're getting closer. That keeps your energy focused and away from distractions. — Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi
cards.action: Set a goal for this session that you can measure in the moment.

cards.title: Memory palace
cards.message: Associate what you want to remember with objects in a house you know well, and walk through it mentally. Visualizing works better than repeating. — Ron White
cards.action: Try memorizing 3 facts by linking them to objects in your home.

cards.title: The inchworm
cards.message: Your performance has a better level and a worse level. Truly improving means raising the worse level, so your whole baseline moves forward. — Jared Tendler
cards.action: Identify your current worst level and pick one concrete improvement for it.

cards.title: Few goals, very ambitious
cards.message: No more than 2 or 3 ambitious goals at a time. Genuine enthusiasm for something that draws you in protects you from trivial distractions. — Cal Newport
cards.action: Trim your goal list down to a maximum of 3.

cards.title: The starting ritual
cards.message: A fixed starting ritual tells your brain it's time to concentrate, and reduces the friction of entering deep focus. — Cal Newport
cards.action: Define a short, repeatable ritual to kick off your focus sessions.

cards.title: Body and mind in balance
cards.message: Athletes need mental discipline to perform; thinkers need physical health to sustain hours of concentration. Body and mind train together. — Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi
cards.action: Add one physical action today, even a short one, to your mental routine.

cards.title: Real vs. illusory confidence
cards.message: Stable confidence rests on proven facts and skills, not lucky streaks. It doesn't depend on short-term results, but on the quality of your process. — Jared Tendler
cards.action: List 1 concrete fact (not a result) that supports your confidence.
